result:
All fixes are implemented and verified (`next build` succeeds, routes render correctly, redirects work). Summary of what changed and why:

**Root cause:** InboxZap (email cleanup tool) pivoted to Lumina (AI meeting assistant), but the migration was left half-done in code. GSC data showed *all 18 homepage clicks* were brand searches for "inboxzap" — landing on a page now 100% about a different product — while 12 legacy email-cleanup blog posts (still pulling ~15k impressions) had become orphaned (the blog index's client-side fetch hit a nonexistent `/api/posts`, so it couldn't even link to them).

**Fixed in `app/layout.tsx`**: title/description/OG tags said "InboxZap" while the actual hero content was Lumina — corrected to match, added `metadataBase`, and added Organization JSON-LD.

**Added `app/features/page.tsx` and `app/pricing/page.tsx`**: linked from nav and listed in the sitemap, but didn't exist in this repo at all (404s).

**Rebuilt the blog** (`lib/posts.ts`, `app/blog/page.tsx`, `app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx`): was a client-only component fetching a nonexistent API — now server-rendered with real per-post routes, `generateMetadata`, and BlogPosting JSON-LD.

**Retired the 12 off-topic legacy posts**: 301'd them to `/blog` in `next.config.js` instead of leaving them 404-bound or orphaned — preserves whatever link equity remains rather than losing it outright. Seeded 4 new posts genuinely on-topic for Lumina (meeting transcription, action-item follow-through, recording comparisons) to start building topical authority in the new niche.

**Replaced the dead `/faq` redirect** (it pointed to `/#faq`, an anchor that didn't exist anywhere) with a real FAQ page, including an explicit "What happened to InboxZap?" entry and FAQPage JSON-LD — since brand searches are the only traffic converting today, that question needs a direct answer rather than a bounce.

**Fixed `sitemap.ts`**: had a literal `// TODO` and only listed 4 static URLs; now includes every real current page and post.

One unrelated flag: `npm install` reported Next.js 14.2.5 has a known security advisory (critical). That's outside this SEO task's scope, but worth patching separately — let me know if you'd like me to bump it.
